content:
Base directory for this skill: /home/user/brushspace/.claude/skills/iwsdk-planner

# IWSDK Project Planner

You are an expert IWSDK (Immersive Web SDK) architect. Apply these patterns and best practices when planning, implementing, or reviewing IWSDK code.

## Core Architecture

IWSDK is built on three pillars:

1. **ECS (Entity Component System)** via `elics` library
2. **Reactive Signals** via `@preact/signals-core`
3. **Three.js Integration** with zero-copy transform binding (super-three v0.181.0)

IWSDK is a 3D web framework with first-class XR support. `World.create()` always creates a persistent `world.player` origin and keeps `world.camera` under it, even when `xr: false` is used for browser-only apps. For first-person browser movement, move `world.player`; for orbit, editor, product, cinematic, or third-person cameras, it is fine to keep `world.player` at the origin and drive `world.camera`. Remember that `world.camera.position` is local to `world.player`, so use `camera.getWorldPosition(...)` when world-space viewer position matters.

Use `input.canvasPointerEvents` for browser mouse/touch canvas events. Browser canvas pointers and XR rays both feed Three/Object3D pointer events and ECS `Hovered`/`Pressed` tags on `Interactable`/`RayInteractable` entities. XR-specific input is available at `world.input.xr`; keyboard and standard browser gamepads live at `world.input.keyboard` and `world.input.browserGamepads`. Reusable systems should prefer `world.input.actions` for 
...[truncated 50357 chars]...
n:**

- button-round.glb at (1, 1, -2), scale 0.5, variation-a texture
- indicator-arrow.glb at (0, 0.1, -2.5), scale 0.3, variation-a texture

result:
Paint in thin air, together.
A WebXR 3D painting app partially ported from Open Brush and adapted for the web, built with the Immersive Web SDK (iwsdk.dev) — no install required.

Live at brushspace.elixr.games — open it in a headset browser (Meta Quest) or any desktop browser.

--- ## Features - **The real brushes** — Open Brush's brush library rendered with the original shaders (audio-reactive ribbons, light, fire, smoke, and friends), ported to run as `RawShaderMaterial`s on WebGL. - **Full painting kit** — free paint and straightedge tools, eraser, color wheel with favorites, dropper, brush size ring, undo/redo, and a camera tool for in-headset snapshots. - **Sketches that persist** — save, browse, and reload sketches locally (IndexedDB), with thumbnails, `.tilt`-compatible documents, and GLB export. - **Two-person collaboration** — host a sketch, speak a six-digit code, and paint together over peer-to-peer WebRTC: live stroke streaming, erase/undo sync, reconnect-and-merge after drops, and a painted bird-head avatar tracking your partner's headset. - **Grab the world** — squeeze both grips to move, rotate, and scale ...[truncated 2180 chars]... layer-canvas-system.ts panel-attachment-system.ts panel-system.ts performance-counter-system.ts runtime-debug-system.ts selection-system.ts sketch-library-system.ts standard-environment-system.ts stroke-authoring-system.ts tip-anchor-tuning-system.ts world-grab-system.ts world-grab-visuals-system.ts

input.query: view only mode viewing sketches without VR monoscopic desktop mode

result:
Title: v2.13 "Lucky Number"
Link: https://docs.openbrush.app/release-history/v2.13
Content: Not enough people realise that Open Brush runs as sketch viewer on all Mac, PC, Android devices even when those devices have no VR support. This release has improved the navigation controls. Touch support on phones is more intuitive and standard gamepads should work as expected. Try installing from Steam on Steam Deck or similar handheld devices.

Furthermore you can now browse more sample sketches which are loaded directly from the Icosa Gallery. Non-VR devices also support interacting with Open Brush via the Web API or monoscopic mode.
Title: Using Open Brush without a VR headset
Link: https://docs.openbrush.app/user-guide/monoscopic-mode
Content: Open Brush has got a secret mode where it can be used without a VR headset - and even works on Mac! This mode was never released to the public and is clunky in places.

It also works very nicely when using Open Brush via the API allowing you to control Open Brush from a web browser.
Title: Feature: New Monoscopic Mode
Link: https://docs.openbrush.app/alternate-and-experimental-builds/old-or-completed-feature-builds/new-monoscopic-mode
Content: At the time of writing - the current beta version has added a new View Sketches mode and has made Monoscopic Mode available without needing to download a separate version of Open Brush. This is probably the direction we will be taking - rather than directly incorporating Rapka's work.

User @rapka on 
...[truncated 4471 chars]...
er that it might be added to the Labs panel in the official release. And finally it would be released as an officially supported feature.

We have merged all those features into the main version now - so there's no need for a separate experimental mode aside from activating the extra set of brushes.

content:
Done — the plan is written up in `docs/plans/non-xr-view-only-mode.md` and pushed to `claude/non-xr-view-only-mode-bfkjwc`. I read the actual Open Brush source (sparse clone of `Assets/Scripts` from `icosa-foundation/open-brush`) rather than the docs. Here's the summary.

**How Open Brush's non-XR viewer works, from the code:**

- **Entry** — `App.Start()` checks `!VrSdk.IsHmdInitialized() && !UserConfig.Flags.EnableMonoscopicMode` and falls back to `CreateFailedToDetectVrDialog()`, which despite the name spawns `InitNoHeadsetMode` — the 2D sketch picker. `UserConfig.Flags.ForceViewOnly` (or the `OPEN_BRUSH_VIEWER` compile define) forces view-only at startup.
- **Picker** (`InitNoHeadsetMode.cs`) — screen-space grid with Your/Featured/Liked tabs; featured sketches download from Icosa in batches of 2 up to 20. Clicking a tile raises the camera to y=12, enables FlyTool, and issues `GlobalCommands.Load`, deliberately bypassing the unsaved-work confirmation. The picker shows "Loading sketch…" until stroke playback begins, then destroys itself so you watch the sketch draw in.
- **View-only state** (`SketchControlsScript.ViewOnly(bool)`, toggled by `GlobalCommands.ViewOnly` / `H` key) — forces FlyTool as the active tool, switches PanelManager to `PanelAvailabilityMode.ViewOnly` (only panels flagged `m_ViewOnly` survive), disables pointer rendering, hides decor. It's a UI/tool gate, not a data mode, and gets re-asserted after every load.
- **Navigation** (`FlyTool.cs` non-VR branch) — look via LMB-drag / one-finger drag / right stick (squared response, pitch clamped ±85°) applied to the **camera**; movement via WASD-style shortcuts / left stick + triggers / an on-screen touch joystick, applied inversely to the **scene pose** with `MakeValidScenePose` bounds clamping, Shift/L3 sprint ×5.
- **Chrome** (`ViewModeUI.cs`) — close (back to picker), menu, and a skip-playback button that calls `RequestQuickLoad()` while playback runs.

**The plan for Brushspace** maps each piece onto what's already here: a new `ViewerModeState` (set by `?view` param and a `navigator.xr` support probe — the param is needed because the dev emulator always claims XR support), a `ViewerNavigationSystem` porting FlyTool's non-VR branch (drag-look + WASD/gamepad move, driving `world.camera` — deliberately *not* the `OpenBrushScenePose`, since that's save/collab state here), a DOM sketch picker reusing `SketchLibrarySystem`'s existing local-IndexedDB + curated-Icosa gallery entries (extracted to a shared module, with `openSketch` made public), and input gating that suppresses the browser paint bindings in view-only. Phased: nav first behind `?view`, then the picker + auto-entry, then touch joystick, then parity extras. The doc also covers the LMB paint-vs-look conflict, camera framing on load, and test strategy.

content:
Everything's wrapped up:

- **Plan pushed** — `docs/plans/non-xr-view-only-mode.md` on `claude/non-xr-view-only-mode-bfkjwc` (commit `0ec5e8c`), covering the Open Brush source analysis (`InitNoHeadsetMode`, `FlyTool`'s non-VR branch, `SketchControlsScript.ViewOnly`, `ViewModeUI`) and the phased Brushspace port (`ViewerModeState`, `ViewerNavigationSystem`, DOM sketch picker reusing the existing gallery entries, input gating).
- **Checkpoint screenshot captured** from inside the emulated Quest 3 session, framing the welcome sketch, wordmark, and the wand gallery panel — the in-VR counterpart of the browser picker the plan proposes.
- **Working tree clean** — session journal committed and pushed (`0d97bbc`); the `package-lock.json` churn from installing deps for the screenshot was reverted since it wasn't part of the work.
does brush space share the architecture of open brush with regards to tools and related modes? should we align them closer to ensure this is maintainable when we add more tools?
Good question — let me look at both sides properly before answering: how Open Brush structures tools (`BaseTool`/`SketchSurfacePanel`), and how Brushspace currently represents them.

content:
Plan corrected and pushed (`00c045c`). Here's the full assessment you asked for.

## You were right about the scene pose — I've retracted that divergence

I went back and checked what `OpenBrushScenePose` actually feeds, and my plan's justification was factually wrong:

- **Collab does not sync the pose.** `CollabSystem.broadcastTip()` converts tips and head poses into canvas space before sending, with a comment saying exactly why: *"Publish in canvas space so each side's world-grab pose stays local."* Strokes travel in canvas space too. The pose is per-user view state — same as Open Brush multiplayer.
- **Save/load never touches it.** Nothing in `src/sketch/` (tilt writer, persistence, catalog) or `SketchLibrarySystem` reads or writes the pose; strokes persist in canvas space.

So `OpenBrushScenePose` is structurally `App.Scene.Pose`: a local world transform with the canvas layers as children, manipulated by world grab, read by stroke authoring for canvas-space conversion, by the eraser for radius scaling, and by collab for coordinate conversion. Navigating by moving the camera would have created a *second* "where is the world" state that composes with the first — exactly the maintainability trap you flagged. The plan now says: translation → scene pose (inverse, with the `MakeValidScenePose`-style clamp), look → camera, which is literally what `FlyTool`'s non-VR branch does. A bonus that falls out for free: fly somewhere in the browser, then Enter VR, and you're where you flew — matching Open Brush's flatscreen→VR behavior.

## Tools and modes: same semantics, different (and appropriate) structure

**Open Brush** is OO: `BaseTool` subclasses with lifecycle virtuals (`EnableTool`/`HideTool`/`UpdateTool`, `InputBlocked` arming, `AvailableDuringLoading`), a single manager (`SketchSurfacePanel.EnableSpecificTool`) owning the active tool, a `ToolType` enum for identity, and separate mode layers (`App.AppState`, `PanelAvailabilityMode`, `m_ViewOnly`) that are *enforced through the tool manager* — view-only works by `EnsureViewOnlyNavigationTool` forcing the current tool into the nav set.

**Brushspace** has translated the same concepts into ECS/data: `OpenBrushToolId` ≈ `ToolType`; the `OpenBrushToolDescriptor` table (`paints`, `erases`, `samplingMode`, `mirrorMode`, …) ≈ `BaseTool`'s capability virtuals; `OpenBrushAppState.activeTool/previousTool/toolRevision` ≈ the manager's state; `resolveEffectiveOpenBrushTool` (straightedge overlaying free-paint) ≈ tool-mode resolution logic. That translation is correct for this codebase — porting the class hierarchy into elics would fight the ECS conventions the project is built on.

**Where real drift will bite as tools grow**, and what I'd align:

1. **No single switch authority.** Panel-system writes `activeTool` and bumps `toolRevision`, but enter/exit side effects (hide eraser cursor, stop audio, reset status) are scattered across the systems that react. One `switchTool()` owner — the `EnableSpecificTool` analog — is the cheapest insurance before the tool count grows.
2. **Capability flags belong on the descriptor.** `StrokeAuthoringSystem` has ~47 `activeTool` references; each new tool means hunting per-tool conditionals across systems. Open Brush encodes "available during loading", "allowed in view-only", "hides pointer" as per-tool virtuals; the descriptor table is the natural home for those as data fields.
3. **Mode→tool policy should stay pure and centralized.** `tool-modes.ts` already does this for straightedge. View-only should extend that same function — `resolveEffectiveOpenBrushTool(tool, { straightEdge, viewOnly })` — rather than sprinkling `viewOnly` checks, which is the direct analog of `IsViewOnlyNavigationTool`/`EnsureViewOnlyNavigationTool`.

All three are now captured in a "Tool-architecture alignment" section of the plan. Short version: **align the semantics (descriptor capabilities, one switch entry point, pure mode-resolution), not the class structure** — that gets you Open Brush's maintainability properties in idiomatic ECS form.
